data.title: Inflation-Proof Your Retirement Income With These 2 Picks
data.description:
It seems that the inflation dragon will once again return to disrupt the markets, our portfolios, and, importantly, the purchasing power of our precious savings (investments).

The war in Iran, which has, among other things, triggered soaring oil prices, will inevitably translate into higher inflation. We still don't have visibility on the level of consequences, but it is clear that we can forget about reaching a long-term 2% inflation target any time soon. The market also agrees with it by basically eliminating interest rate cut probabilities for the remainder of 2026.

The thing is that these dynamics come on top of an already relatively elevated inflation level, which for February 2026 came in at 2.4% (these data points exclude the effects from surging oil prices). Interestingly, exactly the energy component was among the few items that held back the overall CPI from rising even further.

Given the magnitude of change in oil prices (including gas, power and crude oil derivatives), it is a story of not only inflation but also recession, with some of the largest banks already recalibrating the odds for a recession to the upside.

The fact that the overall equity market (SPY) and certain above-average risk areas of the market, such as leveraged fixed income (PDO) and high-growth AI names (QQQ), have been selling off is another sign, which confirms that the problem has two sides: inflation and recession.

For retirement income investors, all this imposes yet another set of headaches.

On the one hand, it would make sense to de-risk (at least on the margin) portfolios from very high-yield names to avoid negative surprises, which might come in case the recession really materializes.

On the other hand, sacrificing yield in the moment, when the purchasing power of in-place current income streams is about to face significant headwinds from rising inflation, is not the smartest idea either (e.g., some might not afford it).

In this article I will elaborate on two picks, which:

  • Offer tangible yields from the start.
  • Carry recession-proof fundamentals.
  • Embody robust inflation pass-through mechanics.

In my view, these are the names that could be deemed as direct beneficiaries from the prevailing macro (and geopolitical) mess and thus have to be at least considered by prudent income investors when thinking about where to deploy incremental capital (or how to reshuffle a bit the existing capital).

#1: BEP, 5.1%

Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BEP)(BEP.UN:CA) is a renewable energy-focused franchise. It develops, owns and operates various renewable asset technologies, which are spread across different geographies (the U.S. is a main one).

The business model is relatively simple: acquire or lease land, get permits, find long-term power offtakers (users) and then, based on that guaranteed contract, source financing and develop the asset base.

What is important to understand from the start is that BEP is not focused on offshore wind, residential solar or renewable technology development. Plus, it does not take market or the so-called merchant risk (i.e., most of the cash flows stem from long-term power purchase agreements, which are contracted with investment-grade corporates).

In the table we can see how this looks in practice:

Besides the cash flow predictability that these contracts bring to the table, they also introduce a timely inflation-indexation factor, which as of Q4 2025 was relevant for ~70% of the total revenues.

So, if inflation goes up (which is very likely), BEP should be able to benefit from that and thus grow its distributions at a faster pace than before.

On top of the embedded CPI escalators, we have to factor in the positive effects from the retained FFO reinvestments (~29% of the total proceeds) and already contracted organic growth pipeline (target is to install 10 GW per year), which will be feeding into cash flow growth going forward.

It is worth noting that BEP has structured its investment grade balance sheet in an optimal way, which allows the business to shield the cash flows from potentially rising borrowing costs, which, theoretically, could offset the benefits from CPI escalators. For example, the corporate debt has a weighted average debt maturity of 13 years, and 100% of it is fixed. The project-level debt has an average term of 10 years, and 96% of it is fixed.

In my view, BEP is nicely positioned on all fronts:

  • It is set to enjoy tailwinds from increased demand and rising prices of power (e.g., think of AI data centers, the need to have off-grid power projects, etc.).
  • The forthcoming inflation is not a risk but an opportunity to enhance the cash flows.
  • The business model is resilient, durable and underpinned by a strong and interest-rate-protected balance sheet.

#2: PAA, 7.7%

Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. Common Units (PAA) is a pure-play midstream infrastructure partnership, which is subject to K-1 forms (just as BEP). However, just as for BEP - Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) - there is an available "non K-1" alternative, Plains GP Holdings (PAGP).

Another thing that these two machines have in common is the durable, resilient and inflation-protected business model.

About 85% of PAA's EBITDA stems from fee-based contracts, which provide long-term cash flow certainty. These contracts come with minimum volume commitments, price risk protection and annual fee escalators.

PAA is also a direct beneficiary of the rising crude oil prices. There are two avenues through which PAA can extract a value from the prevailing dynamics:

  • Each $10/bbl change in WTI price above the ~$62.5 bbl (on this figure the 2026 EBITDA guidance was built) contributes to ~1.5% in incremental EBITDA growth.
  • Each 100 Mb/d change in the Permian Basin production level warrants ~0.375% of incremental EBITDA generation. For example, if the production increases by 10% from the level that was baked into the 2026 guide, the EBITDA would advance by ~4%.

Here it is worth noting that the 2026 guidance itself (excluding the effect from the Iran war or the oil shock) indicates a year-over-year EBITDA growth of 13%.

Currently, it is too early to quantify the impact for PAA's EBITDA, but it is clear that we will most probably be talking about meaningfully stronger results than what was communicated earlier this year.

Now you might wonder what the "so what" is here.

Let's take a look at the chart below:

And then let's consider what the President, CEO & Chairman Willie Chiang said about the MLP's distribution policy in the Q4 2025 earnings call:

With the simplification and streamlining of our business, stable cash flow contributions from the Cactus III acquisition and reduced commodity exposure following the NGL sale, we are modestly reducing our distribution coverage ratio threshold from 160% to 150%. This reflects improved visibility for our business, better aligns us with peers, and it paves the way for future distribution growth while still maintaining a prudent level of coverage.

Already based on the non-adjusted 2026 EBITDA guide PAA had room for a slight distribution hike (i.e., going from 155% to 150% distribution coverage).

Now, with an improving result profile, it would be only reasonable to expect juicier distribution hikes.

Finally, PAA carries an investment-grade balance sheet with a well laddered debt maturity profile, where the lion's share of borrowings are based on fixed rates. In other words, the partnership is nicely positioned to reap the benefit for its top-line growth, while being largely unaffected on the cost of debt side.

Final remarks

The investment picks that I presented above are certainly solid choices to fight back inflation with current income growth. More importantly, both picks are supported by durable business models and investment grade balance sheets that come in handy in the context of potential recession.

There is a difference in yields between what BEP and PAA offer.

For BEP the yield is lower because it has a more growth-oriented strategy (e.g., huge development pipeline, active asset rotation process to free up capital for growth funding etc.), which always comes with a bit higher multiples.

PAA has lower structural growth possibilities (crude oil, which before the war with Iran was assumed to be oversupplied), but right now, due to the unexpected oil shock, it could potentially register even bigger distribution hikes than BEP (at least in the near term or until the oil prices normalize).

In a nutshell, I would suggest prudent income investors seriously consider integrating these picks into their portfolios for stress-free inflation protection.

data.title: Bitcoin: What's Really Happening Despite The Bear Market
data.description:
I think Bitcoin offers a rather favorable setup today because the price has reset far more than the adoption has. Bitcoin no longer trades like a euphoric asset, yet the buyer base keeps getting broader.

What Drives Bitcoin Today

Bitcoin still comes down to scarce new supply meeting variable but growing demand. 21 million BTC will ever exist. Less than a million are left to be mined over the next century.

Demand is no longer confined to niche exchanges and retail speculation. Traditional brokers are offering BTC and other crypto, and even including the option to withdraw to self custody.

The SEC approved spot Bitcoin ETPs on January 10, 2024, and the product set has since matured into a large pool of regulated access. IBIT’s current size on its own is enough to show that this is not a passing experiment. What’s even more remarkable is that IBIT’s options market has started to surpass Deribit in open interest and volume. Deribit is the offshore crypto options exchange that has dominated the crypto options scene for years. Combine this with the growth of CME futures on BTC and we see that the U.S. regulated derivatives complex for Bitcoin is signaling increased demand by more traditional institutions.

Corporate balance sheets are now part of that demand stack too, and this is perhaps the most underappreciated even though it is the most important. Strategy remains the main case, but it is no longer alone. Over 2025, the number of new public companies holding BTC doubled.

Almost half a million BTC—equivalent to half the remaining supply to be released over the next century—was purchased by public corporations in 2025. This does not include private companies or institutional investors.

The important thing is Bitcoin does not need every public company to become MicroStrategy. A widening corporate buyer universe is enough to drive the price much higher.

The question of course is why BTC still dropped 13% in 2025 even with so much buying. The answer is that a lot of early Bitcoin buyers were selling into this demand. Ultimately, price is set by supply and demand, and we saw an episode where the selling was stronger than the buying. What makes Bitcoin different is that there is a hard limit to how much the early BTC investors can sell, and some of the largest corporate buyers do not intend to ever sell (more on this in the next section), so the supply and demand dynamic is constantly one of moving BTC from weak hands into strong hands, which will likely lead to a supply shock.

What the Market is Missing

I think the most underappreciated near-term demand lever is Strategy’s preferred-stock machine, especially STRC. STRC is now a $5 billion asset even though it launched several months ago with a $2.5 billion IPO. Strategy issued another $2.5 billion in recent months via its ATM program, signaling the demand for this preferred stock.

But what exactly is STRC and why is it so interesting to investors?

First, I wrote a long article about it a few months ago. To keep it short, it's a preferred stock that adjusts its dividend monthly to keep the price stable at about $100 per share. There’s obviously lots of caveats to this and economic reflexivity we don’t have to get into, but the main point is that because the dividend is meant to hold the price stable, it pays a pretty high dividend. Currently 11.5%. Furthermore, due to Strategy’s negative taxable earnings and profits, the dividends are treated as return of capital, which means they go towards lowering your cost basis, effectively deferring the tax liability until you sell the stock.

Something that pays 11.5% tax-deferred while maintaining a generally stable price is clearly interesting to people. This is why they were able to double STRC’s notional value outstanding after the IPO (which was also the largest IPO in 2025). The bottom line is that when Strategy sells STRC, it takes the money to buy BTC. So from this alone, we have a good way to think about Bitcoin buy side demand:

  • How much do people want something like STRC?

  • How much of the STRC acquisition will be met by Strategy selling it rather than an existing holder selling it?

  • How much STRC can Strategy sell?

Note that I do not assume all STRC buying translates one-for-one into future Bitcoin buying by Strategy, but it is enough to say the marginal-buyer pipeline is now larger than most investors appreciate. STRC buyers are basically “partial” Bitcoin buyers that buy BTC by proxy via Strategy. And the extent of the word “partial” depends on how much STRC is being sold by Strategy vs existing holders of STRC.

Consider that in June 2025 there was no STRC. Today, $5 billion has been sold, so another $5 billion of BTC has been bought by Strategy. Without STRC, much of that $5 billion would have gone into other assets. This, I feel, is a good abstract model to understand the relationship between STRC and Bitcoin demand.

STRC is part of a broader pattern of what is increasingly being referred to as “Digital Credit”. A company will endeavor to buy BTC and then issue credit instruments against it to buy more BTC. If BTC appreciates at a faster rate than the coupon paid on the credit, then the company is creating shareholder value. The credit instrument is called “Digital Credit” because it is backed by Bitcoin, which is considered “Digital Capital.”

For Digital Credit issuers, the goal is to never sell BTC. If over a multi-year time horizon, BTC continues to outperform, then it is possible to issue Digital Credit forever against an increasing NAV in Digital Capital. The proceeds from issuance are used to buy more BTC. Eventually, if Digital Credit issuers buy up almost all the BTC that people are willing to sell, then each additional purchase will continue to drive the price higher, which enables more credit to be issued.

There’s some valid arguments for the weaknesses of this theory, but it's important to note that all the issuers today are heavily overcollateralized. They own multiples more BTC than their outstanding credit. Some even have large cash reserves to instill confidence in their ability to cover dividends for an extended stretch of Bitcoin market weakness.

So to summarize, I view the development and scale of STRC and other digital credit instruments as a very important development that has grown the pipeline into BTC. In the medium term, this will likely be more important than brokers offering BTC. And here is what I mean:

If people do not want to buy BTC, they are unlikely to change their mind in the near future. But people are interested in fixed income and yield. Digital Credit is about channeling BTC through a corporate structure to deliver that fixed income or yield in a regulated form that people recognize. Today, STRC is the most liquid preferred stock in the world.

On-Chain Signals

The on-chain setup is good. Bitbo’s MVRV metric says Bitcoin has traded below 1.0 only about 15% of trading days, while values above 2.4 have historically occupied the hotter part of the cycle. Today’s 1.268 MVRV means the average holder is sitting on roughly a 27% unrealized gain, not the kind of broad speculative excess that usually defines a final top.

Bitbo’s realized price is about $54,342, so a full reset to 1.0x realized value would point to the mid-$50,000s, while a move back to the 2.4x band would point to roughly $130,000. In other words, Bitcoin is much closer to fair-value support than anything else.

That is why I still like the long side here. By my reading, Bitcoin sits in the cheaper end of its historical on-chain range. Bitcoin has repriced sharply lower from its October 6, 2025 all-time high, yet the institutional channels that matter most have remained, if not strengthened.

Other Catalysts

I think it is interesting to discuss other catalysts. This gives the reader a broader view of what else is happening.

The first catalyst is continued corporate adoption. I do not think the market has fully internalized the second-order effect of boardrooms treating Bitcoin treasury policy as repeatable. Consider how Square has rolled out Bitcoin merchant infrastructure to its U.S. customer base. This could cause a lot more smaller businesses and upper-middle class individuals to consider BTC.

The second catalyst is market structure. CME said its regulated crypto futures and options will move to 24/7 trading beginning May 29, pending regulatory review. It also said 2026 year-to-date crypto derivatives volume reached 407,200 contracts of ADV, up 46% year over year. This really matters because weekend gap risk and hedging frictions have always limited how traditional firms engage with BTC. CME’s new direction will ameliorate this issue.

The third catalyst is regulatory capital treatment. The Basel Committee’s current crypto standard, implemented from January 1, 2026, still leaves most bitcoin exposures in a conservative bucket, including a 1% Tier 1 exposure threshold for Group 2 and punitive 1250% risk-weight examples for Group 2b. But Basel also said in February 2026 that it has expedited a targeted review of the cryptoasset standard and will provide an update later this year. I would not build a BTC thesis on a Basel rewrite, but even incremental relief would widen the buyer base inside banks and bank-adjacent intermediaries.

Risks

I think the biggest short-term risk is that the old four-year cycle still dominates. Bitcoin’s last all-time high was October 6, 2025. If that was the cycle peak, and if the usual post-peak bear phase again lasts around a year, then the more painful part of the downside would not end until roughly October 2026. The on-chain support zone I mentioned gives a framework to understand that we are currently in a relatively safer buy zone, but there is no guarantee that this region is the true bottom. A retest of realized price would imply the mid-$50,000s, and a deeper washout toward the kind of undervaluation 0.85 MVRV would point to the mid-$40,000s.

The second risk is that the Strategy funding flywheel slows before the Bitcoin repricing arrives. STRC looks stronger than many skeptics expected, but the entire mechanism still depends on capital markets access. If demand for these instruments fades, then a meaningful marginal buyer disappears. The thesis can survive without Strategy, but the near-term torque is probably much lower.

Conclusion

I think the balance of evidence still favors upside from here. I rate BTC a Strong Buy. The network looks healthy, corporate and ETF adoption remain real, and Strategy’s STRC complex has matured into a meaningful conduit for incremental Bitcoin demand.

data.title: Bonds Need To Confirm Stock Optimism
data.description:
When the war in Iran began at the beginning of this month, I asserted that markets would dictate policy and determine when it ended similar to the way markets forced the President to backtrack on his punitive tariffs announced last April. I expected a de-escalation before the end of the month. On Friday, the market’s message was clear, as all risk assets plunged in value with stocks, bonds, precious metals, and cryptocurrencies cascading, while oil prices rose sharply. Investors had had enough and seemed to be buckling down for the possibility of a bear market and recession to follow. The most jarring decline was in Treasuries where yields surged, indicating a rate increase from the Fed later this year. Regardless, the President escalated his rhetoric on Saturday evening with a threat to destroy Iran’s power plants in 48 hours if they did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz, clearly not recognizing that such an act would constitute a war crime.

On Monday morning, markets were ready to pick up where they left off at Friday’s close in a sea of red when the President had a sudden change of heart around 7:00 am. Evidently, while he was threatening to destroy Iran’s power plants he was also having productive conversations to end the war in the Middle East. That was all investors needed to hear to buy the dip, as the major market indexes soared, and WTI crude plunged $10, but the bond market was more suspect.

Within an hour of the President’s Truth Social post, Iranian officials said there had been no negotiations, and that Trump manufactured the news to influence oil and financial markets. Stock investors didn’t seem to care, as the President’s statement was a clear sign of his desire to de-escalate, whether or not the Iranians were involved or on board. Yet this may have been why the bond market failed to rally on Monday. The 10-year yield pulled back from its peak on Friday but failed to retrace the prior trading day’s surge.

The 2-year yield saw similar action and remains above the Fed Funds rate, which indicates continued concerns about a rate hike later this year. It is clear that bond investors need action more than words, which ultimately results in a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. This tells me that we are likely to see more volatility this week and perhaps a couple more down days in stocks, but I think Monday was a critical turning point in the conflict that should put the peak in oil prices behind us.

If oil has peaked, then the broad market has likely bottomed. Granted, even if oil prices edge lower, they will remain elevated above pre-war levels for a period of time. Furthermore, the increase in chemicals and other raw materials that also pass through the strait have disrupted supply chains, which will elevate prices for everything from food to electronics to manufacturing and transportation costs. Still, if the market sees the other end of this disruption later this year, the strong fundamentals we had upon entering this conflict should sustain the economic expansion and bull market.

The war accelerated a much needed revaluation in the technology sector that was already underway with the Magnificent 7 now having pulled back a collective 15% from their all-time high, while the Nasdaq has completed a 10% correction. Yet the average stock has fared much better over the past month. The equally weighed S&P 500 held its long-term moving average, as has the Russell 2000 index, which I view as positive.

data.title: Protagonist Partners With Johnson & Johnson To Compete With AbbVie's Skyrizi
data.description:
Last Wednesday, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced that the FDA had approved Icotyde, a once-daily oral peptide for the treatment of plaque psoriasis. This drug selectively blocks the interleukin-23 receptor (IL-23) and was a joint discovery with Protagonist Therapeutics (PTGX). The drug has also been submitted for approval to the European Medicines Agency (EMA), and the US launch is already underway.

Upon learning this news, the market reacted by penalizing AbbVie (ABBV), with the stock trading approximately 5% lower since. AbbVie is the maker of an injectable IL-23 blocker named Skyrizi, which is the drugmaker's best-selling drug, achieving 2025 total sales of $17.6B. The 5% drop in AbbVie's stock represents a loss in market capitalization of approximately $28 billion (a $16/share drop times 1.77 billion shares outstanding).

The clear winner of this development is Protagonist. The FDA approval triggered a $50M milestone payment from JNJ with potentially $580M in future milestones and a 6-10% royalty on sales of Icotyde. While the profit-sharing agreement limits upside to some extent, it also places the costs of commercialization with JNJ.

Protagonist has additional commercialization catalysts that may be realized this year, specifically Rusfertide, a first-in-class hepcidin mimetic that targets red blood cell overproduction for the treatment of polycythemia vera (PV). The company has secured Priority Review with the FDA on the basis of the drug's Phase 3 VERIFY trial that met all primary and secondary endpoints. This drug is being developed with Takeda with a PDUFA date in Q3 2026 and potential commercialization thereafter.

Protagonist's platform peptide technology presents the company with a strong pipeline of additional clinical candidates in obesity, hematology, and inflammation-related diseases. Analysts see the company achieving profitability this year from milestone payments, with more durable profitability by 2028 as commercialization progresses. Finally, JNJ has been in talks to acquire Protagonist, offering investors a potential bonus if a deal is reached and some degree of downside protection.

To summarize, progress has been robust, and on the basis of commercialization and other catalysts, Protagonist earns a Buy rating.

Icotyde Conference Call and Commercialization Implications

Following FDA approval, Protagonist hosted a conference call to discuss the implications. The company's CEO, Dinesh Patel, began the call by stressing the unique mechanism of action for the drug. Icotyde is the first and only targeted oral peptide designed to block the IL-23 receptor. This is a contrast to drugs like Skyrizi that are injectable antibodies that work by targeting the IL-23 ligand.

This results in efficacy that is described as 'best-in-class' for an oral drug and safety that is described as 'placebo-like,' indicating that Icotyde should prove competitive with existing therapies. Skyrizi still holds the absolute crown for efficacy, while Icotyde is the only oral therapy. It remains to be seen how differentiated an oral daily pill will be versus a quarterly subcutaneous injection.

Metric (16 Weeks)* Skyrizi Icotyde Comparison
Mechanism IL-23 inhibitor IL-23 receptor antagonist Direct inhibitor vs. receptor
Dosing Subcutaneous Injection (quarterly) Oral Pill (once daily) Icotyde - Oral advantage
PASI 90 Response 75% 65% Skyrizi
PASI 100 Response 51% 40% Skyrizi
sPGA/IGA 0 or 1 88% 74% Skyrizi
Any Adverse Event 50% (comparable to placebo) 46% (comparable to placebo) Comparable
Serious Adverse Events Very low ~0.4% Very low ~1.1% within placebo Comparable
Boxed Warnings None None Comparable

*Data for Skyrizi and Icotyde from UltIMMa-1/2 and Iconic-Lead studies, respectively. Skyrizi data are at 16 weeks, while Icotyde is at 24 weeks. Some efficacy data were slightly lower for Icotyde at 16 weeks.

Protagonist had earned a total of $337.5M in milestone payments. The March 18th FDA approval officially triggered an additional $50M payout, bringing Protagonist's total earned milestones to $387.5M. An additional $580M in milestones can be achieved through expansion of the approval in addition to milestones based on net sales.

Protagonist will also receive tiered royalties on net worldwide Icotyde sales, at percentages ranging from 6% to 10%. On the first $4B in sales, the average royalty rate is 7.25%, and above that it is 10%.

If Icotyde reaches sales comparable to Skyrizi ($17.6B in 2025), it would reach annual royalties of $1.66B. This would be a best-case result, with Citi and Jefferies modeling peak sales estimates of $5.5B and $7.5B. JNJ has stated that the drug has $5B+ peak sales potential.

Assuming that Protagonist achieves the low-end estimates of $5B in Icotyde sales, this would generate royalties of $400M annually. At the company's current $6.31B market capitalization, the stock is trading at 15.8x this single, high-margin future royalty stream.

Rusfertide Timeline and Potential Commercialization Implications

The next major commercialization catalyst will arrive in Q3 2026 when the FDA will render a decision on Rusfertide, a first-in-class investigational injectable mimetic of the natural hormone hepcidin for the rare blood disorder PV. Protagonist entered into a worldwide license and collaboration agreement with Takeda (TAK) for this drug.

Under the agreement (2025 10K page 6), Takeda and Protagonist take a 50/50 share of profits and losses for the drug, with tiered royalties (from 10-17%) on ex-US sales. However, Protagonist has the right to opt out of the collaboration agreement in the US and currently expects to exercise that right by Q2 2026.

In this case, Takeda will have an exclusive worldwide license to develop and commercialize the drug, providing royalties ranging from 14% to 29% on annual sales, with an average royalty rate of 21% at $1.5B in net sales and 29% above $1.5B. The reason that the company has signaled it will do this is that it insulates the company from the massive costs of launching the drug.

Under these circumstances, Protagonist will receive up to $400M for exercising the opt-out, totaling an aggregate of $975M in development, regulatory, and sales milestones.

The data obtained for Rusfertide showed durable responses in a Phase 3 study (VERIFY) in patients with PV.

Key findings from the study include:

  • The primary endpoint was met with 77% of patients responding compared to 33% on placebo (p<0.0001).
  • The first secondary endpoint was met, with a mean of 0.5 phlebotomies per patient on drug vs. 1.8 per patient on placebo (p<0.0001)
  • Three other key secondary endpoints were met with statistical significance.
  • The drug was generally well-tolerated, with no new safety findings observed during the study.

While FDA approval is never guaranteed, the data are very positive. Protagonist has predicted peak sales of between $1B and $2B. At the midpoint, this implies royalties of 21% or $315M annually if sales peak around $1.5B.

Protagonist's Pipeline and Platform Technology

To understand why Protagonist commands a premium valuation, investors need to look under the hood at the company's proprietary Vectrix platform. This was originally developed in the Australian laboratory of Mark Smythe, a co-founder of the company. It allows for the de novo selection of naturally occurring peptide starting points that are optimized into peptide drugs that are often orally dosed. Both Icotyde and Rusfertide were discovered via this platform.

The technology is built to exploit the advantages of constrained peptides, which are much smaller than antibody-based drugs; thus, they are amenable to oral delivery. Icotyde and Rusfertide have proven the platform and delivered new drugs; however, numerous newer clinical candidates are at earlier stages of development.

These candidate drugs include PN-881, an oral IL-17 antagonist expected to complete Phase 1 studies in 2H 2026. PN-477, an obesity drug poised for clinical proof of concept in the same time frame, as well as PN-8047 and PN-458 poised to enter Phase 1 development this year.

The FDA approval of Icotyde is the ultimate validation of this platform. It proves that Protagonist can successfully generate an oral drug for a validated biologic target.

While it is impossible to predict which drug candidates will be successful, the platform is performing impeccably. With two successful examples, Protagonist seems poised to create value for shareholders with additional constrained peptide drugs in the coming years.

Conclusion

The market's reaction to Icotyde's FDA approval penalized AbbVie but strangely did not lead to a rally in shares of Protagonist or JNJ. Both cannot be true: if AbbVie loses sales due to this catalyst, they must be moved to Icotyde. Skyrizi still enjoys a slight efficacy advantage; the availability of oral IL-23 drugs should grow the patient pool because needle-hesitant patients now have an oral alternative. This bodes well for both JNJ and Protagonist; however, it should be of more material benefit to the smaller company.

In the coming year, Protagonist has an exceptional number of commercialization catalysts. Icotyde has two additional Phase 3 studies due to report in May 2026 and February 2027 for psoriatic arthritis and additional studies in ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease due by 2028. This could massively expand the drug's potential peak sales.

Furthermore, the upcoming Q3 2026 PDUFA date for Rusfertide provides a near-term commercialization catalyst in PV that seems very likely to succeed given the reported Phase 3 data from the VERIFY trial. In the longer term, additional clinical candidates in obesity, hematology, and inflammation-related diseases may create significant value for shareholders.

While the stock has enjoyed a robust rally over the past year, the sheer volume of de-risked, high-margin cash flow coming online in the next 12 to 24 months suggests the current valuation is still conservative. For investors looking for a derisked biotech with blockbuster royalty exposure, elite big-pharma partnerships, and a validated discovery engine, Protagonist Therapeutics represents a compelling Buy.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Waiting Is Hard
data.description:
Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) has been trading sideways to lower after a run into year's end. The chart below showcases the stock on the six-month chart.

RKLB stock had a breakout moment in mid-January, hitting almost $100 per share, and it’s been pretty much downhill from there. Rocket Lab is down about 13% over the past three months.

War aside, the factor weighing on the stock's performance on good and bad days is the long-awaited Neutron rocket. For reference, this is the next-gen, partially reusable, medium-lift, two-stage launch rocket. It’s a real step up from the current Electron, which can deliver 300 kg, as the Neutron can deliver up to 13K kg to low Earth orbit, equipped with nine Archimedes engines, a vacuum-optimized Archimedes on stage two, and a Hungry Hippo fairing design. To us, Rocket Lab’s upside will come from Neutron. This isn’t to say it’s as simple as buying the stock and waiting for Neutron, because waiting has proven to be riddled with booby traps, specifically Neutron delays. It is, instead, to say that all the ingredients are there, and it smells like it's going to be great once it's ready.

Neutron: When will it happen?

To understand why we say “long-awaited” Neutron, it's important to take a step back and look at how long the Neutron has been coming. The Neutron was first announced in March 2021, with a first launch expected in 2024. In late 2023, the 2024 target launch was abandoned and pushed until 2025 due to slower-than-expected development of the Archimedes engine. Come 1H25, Bleecker Street Research published a short-seller report that argued the Neutron wouldn’t launch until mid-2026 or later. Rocket Lab pushed out its Neutron launch to the second half of the year. By mid-2025, Rocket Lab stuck to its plan for a 2025 launch until the Q3 print, when CEO Peter Beck pushed the target date again to 1Q26 to get the Neutron to the launchpad and expected the actual launch to follow thereafter. The stock took a hit on each of the aforementioned setbacks. The Q3 pushout essentially pushed out the profitability timeline, too. A launch pushed from 2025 to 2026 meant that the five launches per year target would be pushed out as well, meaning the earnings from Neutron would come later than expected. This year, we got more hurdles with a tank failure at a joint by a third-party contractor in January, and then in the company’s Q4 print, the Neutron was pushed again to 4Q26. The largest stock moves have been triggered by Neutron headlines, positive or negative. So, investors have grown more or less desensitized to Neutron delays; the most indicative signal of this is the lackluster reaction to the pushout earlier this year compared to the reaction we saw last November.

The neutron isn’t an easy feat; the design and capabilities this rocket would bring to Rocket Lab would be nothing short of a game-changer. The Hungry Hippo design, for example, allows the rocket to be reusable by keeping fairings on after the first stage instead of throwing them out, solving the problem faced in rockets such as SpaceX’s Falcon 9, where the payload fairing gets dumped in two halves once the given rocket has ascended high enough, and then SpaceX is faced with the tricky task of catching or fishing them out of wherever they've fallen.

The Neutron has the potential to materially re-rate Rocket Lab’s launch service revenue, which has been growing as a percentage of total revenue but remains stubbornly below the real revenue moat, which is still its space systems. The former constitutes about 26% of the backlog, while space systems make up 74%. The company’s launch services now consist of the Electron rocket, a small-lift rocket that accounts for about 42% of Rocket Lab’s total revenue. It’s the leading option within the small launch market and holds a launch cadence of one rocket every 11-13 days. Aside from the Electron, Rocket Lab also has HASTE, or Hypersonic Accelerator Suborbital Test Electron. This is a specialized suborbital launch vehicle for affordable flight testing of hypersonic systems, and it’s built on the Electron itself, but with a second-stage modification to carry hypersonic test payloads, as much as 700 kg, instead of deploying satellites to orbit. HASTE is specifically best thought of as Rocket Lab’s direct exposure to defense. The company is positioning itself well with the Department of Defense and Golden Dome ambitions, inking a $190M contract for 20 HASTE launches. HASTE has a higher ASP than the Electron, but Neutron’s ASP takes the win for most attractive. The estimated ASP per launch for Electron is about $8M, HASTE at $12M to $15M, and Neutron at $50-$55M. The wait has been long, but once Neutron is up, the financials should follow closely. Adding a bit more cushion is the fact that Electron’s ASP should be on the rise, considering that Electron launch revenue was up 44% Y/Y in Q3, versus costs per launch up less at only 18%.

The Neutron’s most recent delay triggers no major alarms for us. The company has an automated machine, AFP, that builds tanks, and as this machine was being commissioned, Rocket Lab worked with a third-party contractor to build a test tank. This test tank had a defect that caused it to rupture during testing, indicating a manufacturing rather than a design issue. Beck said:

The tank did meet its anticipated flight loads, but as we prepared to open up the test bounds and push the pressures and loads beyond this to understand the margins and the structure, the tank let go earlier than we expected

So, to us, this does not read as alarming, and instead it reads as what it is, which is a longer waiting period for something that’ll change Rocket Lab’s business in the years to come and the space market itself. Bears will correctly point out a pattern of delays forming. We think it’s a mistake to link this pattern to credibility concerns this early on. CFO Adam Spice pointed out that the delay “gave every other subsystem team the opportunity to really fully exorcise all the demons, much more than they could have under the compressed time schedule they were working towards.” More importantly, we’re talking about a high-penetration market, and Neutron will be going up against Falcon 9. This is no secret. Spice has publicly said, “We are positioning Neutron to compete directly with the Falcon 9.” Neutron is still behind Falcon 9 on some fronts, including available capacity, but Rocket Lab is betting on knowing the market itself, in which, as of the current backdrop, most commercial customers don’t need huge capacity, or twice the size that Falcon 9 provides. In fact, customer LEO launches average about 3,400 kg per payload, still comfortably within Neutron’s capacity range. So, the advantage could be with Rocket Lab having a cheaper solution versus Falcon 9 for the amount of capacity customers really require. Falcon 9’s ASP is about $70M, comfortably higher than Rocket Lab’s target ASP of $50-$55M for Neutron. There are other competitors in the medium-lift space apart from SpaceX, including Blue Origin’s new Glenn or even Firefly. Yet, we single out SpaceX here because that is where the monopoly resides. We don’t see Rocket Lab dethroning SpaceX, but we think it can bring diversification to customers who have been missing from space.

Risks: Growth isn’t free

Last week, Rocket Lab announced that it is entering into an equity distribution agreement to sell as much as $1B of its common stock over time, with the firms involved being BofA Securities, BTIG, Cantor Fitzgerald, Citizens JMP Securities, Deutsche Bank Securities, Goldman Sachs, KeyBanc Capital Markets, Morgan Stanley, Needham & Company, Roth Capital Partners, and Stifel Nicolaus & Company. The reason Rocket Lab is doing this is that it’s burning through cash, $45M to $50M quarterly, as it attempts to transition to the next period of growth: Neutron. For investors, this is an ugly headline to see pop up, particularly after another Neutron delay, because it means we could still see cash burn increase before Neutron launches. The optics of this make it look like management is in a less comfortable position than hoped. Adding to the mixed reaction is the risk of share dilution for a company with a $40B market cap, $600M in revenue, and no profit. Valuation is of relevance here as well, with the stock trading at a forward EV/Sales of 44.04x, compared with a group average of 2.09x. Price/Sales is also very comfortably ahead of the group average by about 3,174%. We take the valuation metrics with a grain of salt because, in our opinion, they would only be meaningful if we had the right peer group, whereas now Rocket Lab is being compared to mature industrial names. Being drawn away from Rocket Lab for its current valuation is equivalent to turning away from Tesla back in 2012.

We think the stock trading down in reaction to last week's headline is only normal, but we don’t think it should be viewed as a panic signal. The core business remains very much intact, as discussed above, and to us it reads as management being proactive, not desperate, raising capital preemptively rather than out of need. The $1B figure is also not an overnight commitment; there is room for management to use part of it or none. Even if we see the full amount exercised over time, we think the backlog's compounding growth rate of the business itself justifies the number.

Where do we stand on Rocket Lab?

We’ve been covering the stock since May of last year, when it traded at around $21 per share. The stock is comfortably up since then, about 216% versus the S&P 500, up 13% over the same period. Since our most recent buy call, shown below, the stock is up about 40% against a flat S&P 500. Rocket Lab is easily one of the most volatile stocks at the moment, with a Beta of 2.21 and a 52-week range between $14.71 and $99.58.

Part of why the stock moves this way has a lot to do with the Neutron, but also with broader space-related conversation. We saw the stock move favorably on SpaceX IPO talks earlier this year, and again, we saw it move up on Nvidia’s GTC announcement of Space-1 Vera Rubin to take AI data centers into orbit. Our view of Rocket Lab is that of a long-term pick, and so we advise investors not to get consumed by the volatility and focus on the long-term potential at hand and use volatility to accumulate towards that. At current levels, the stock is hovering around the high $60s range and has been finding support within this range since early February. RSI has similarly locked into a 40s range. The support level it is sitting on now is the EMA21 on the weekly chart. We advise longer-term investors to add to their position on any pullback because we think the Rocket Lab story is just getting started.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager Lifestyle Aggressive Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The majority of the benefit from allocation came from the fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. The fund's real assets portfolio also contributed to relative performance, primarily as a result of a position in metals and mining stocks. The sector performed very well in the quarter thanks to impressive rallies in both precious and industrial metals. An overweight in equities versus bonds was a further plus. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. mid caps detracted.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.
